elements.sent-2411-001: This paper presents an underwater acoustic reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (UA-RIS) designed for long-range, high-speed, and environmentally friendly communication in oceanic environments.
elements.sent-2411-002: The proposed UA-RIS comprises multiple pairs of acoustic reflectors that utilize in-phase and quadrature (IQ) modulation to flexibly control the amplitude and phase of reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-003: This capability enables precise beam steering to enhance or attenuate sound levels in specific directions.
elements.sent-2411-004: A prototype UA-RIS with 4$\times$6 acoustic reflection units is constructed and tested in both tank and lake environments to evaluate performance.
elements.sent-2411-005: The experimental results indicate that the prototype is capable of effectively pointing reflected waves to targeted directions while minimizing side lobes using passive IQ modulation.
elements.sent-2411-006: Field tests reveal that deploying the UA-RIS on the sender side considerably extends communication ranges by 28\% in deep water and 46\% in shallow waters.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, with a fixed communication distance, positioning the UA-RIS at the transmitter side substantially boosts data rates, with an average increase of 63.8\% and peaks up to 96\%.
elements.sent-2411-008: When positioned on the receiver side, the UA-RIS can expand the communication range in shallow and deep water environments by 40.6\% and 66\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-009: Moreover, placing the UA-RIS close to the receiver enhances data rates by an average of 80.3\%, reaching up to 163\% under certain circumstances.
elements.sent-2411-010: In recent years, radio frequency reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RF-RIS) have emerged as a promising technology to enhance communication quality for 5G and beyond networks~\cite{yang2023beyond .}
elements.sent-2411-011: By dynamically adjusting the amplitude and phase of reflected electromagnetic waves with the load network, RIS can precisely steer beams in specific directions.
elements.sent-2411-012: This capability enhances signal strength at the receiver while reducing interference in surrounding environments, thereby improving the overall performance of wireless communication networks~\cite{wu2024intelligent .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Although extensive research has been conducted for RIS in radio environments, bringing this cutting-edge technology into underwater environments remains a complex problem.
elements.sent-2411-014: In water, the transmission range of electromagnetic (EM) waves is severely restricted due to significant absorption attenuation.
elements.sent-2411-015: Currently, acoustic communication is still the principal method for mid-range and long-range communication in marine settings.
elements.sent-2411-016: However, the physical properties of acoustic waves are completely distinct from those of radio signals, rendering existing RF-RIS unsuitable for direct application in water.
elements.sent-2411-017: At present, only limited studies explore the design of underwater acoustic RIS (UA-RIS)~\cite{sun2022high, wang2023designing .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Our work demonstrates that UA-RIS can significantly extend communication range, enhance data rates, and optimize channel utilization for underwater acoustic networks.
elements.sent-2411-019: Nevertheless, to transition UA-RIS from theory to practical application, we must overcome a series of distinct challenges: \begin{adjustwidth {-0.71cm {0cm.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Constrained energy supply: UA-RIS systems operate in maritime settings, where stable power infrastructure is often absent.
elements.sent-2411-021: Consequently, UA-RIS units, especially those situated on the ocean floor, require energy harvesting from sources such as ocean waves or microorganisms for self-sufficiency~\cite{kelly2023prototyping .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Nonetheless, the power density provided by these sustainable sources tends to be low, presenting substantial challenges for UA-RIS design.
elements.sent-2411-023: Addressing this limitation necessitates meticulous planning in both system design and power management strategy.
elements.sent-2411-024: High dynamic range of acoustic signal: To mitigate the high propagation attenuation over long range, acoustic modems are designed with high source levels.
elements.sent-2411-025: As a result, the intensity of sound waves reaching the UA-RIS varies widely with the distance.
elements.sent-2411-026: This high dynamic strength of incident waves poses a substantial challenge for system development.
elements.sent-2411-027: The reflection unit must be able to handle both weak and strong signals, requiring versatile load network configurations to accurately reflect waves with the intended amplitude and phase.
elements.sent-2411-028: Low acoustic frequency: The frequency employed for mid-range and long-range acoustic communication is significantly lower than that of RF systems due to the substantial frequency-dependent absorption of acoustic waves in water.
elements.sent-2411-029: Consequently, the wavelength of acoustic signals becomes long.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this context, the phase of the reflected waves cannot be adjusted by changing the length of the transmission line, a method commonly applied in RF-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-031: UA-RIS necessitates more efficient phase-shift mechanisms to effectively control wave reflection.
elements.sent-2411-032: Each pair of reflectors then performs in-phase and quadrature (IQ) modulation to direct the reflected beam in specific directions.
elements.sent-2411-033: Compared to the 1-bit and 2-bit coding schemes widely used in RF-RIS for beam steering~\cite{kim2023rotated, yang2023beyond , the IQ modulation offers significantly enhanced flexibility.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Specifically, traditional multi-bit coding schemes can produce only a finite number of phase states.
elements.sent-2411-035: This limitation results in coarse control over the reflected waves, leading to less precise beam direction.
elements.sent-2411-036: Furthermore, multi-bit coding schemes typically do not allow altering the amplitude of the reflected wave, resulting in higher side lobes in the beam pattern, which can cause unexpected interference issues.
elements.sent-2411-037: In contrast, IQ modulation enables the formation of reflected waves with arbitrary amplitude and phase, supporting more sophisticated array processing methods such as minimum variance distortionless response (MVDR) and linearly constrained minimum variance (LCMV)~\cite{van2002optimum .}
elements.sent-2411-038: As a result, the reflected waves can be accurately directed in the desired directions without introducing strong interferences in environments.
elements.sent-2411-039: A prototype equipped with 24 (6 $\!\!\times\!\!$ 4) acoustic reflectors is implemented to assess the effectiveness of the proposed UA-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-040: Each reflector is constructed with a Tonpilz structure to reflect incident waves efficiently at 28\,kHz frequency.
elements.sent-2411-041: The complete assembly, which includes the matching circuit and the load network, is controlled by an ultra-low-power microcontroller (MCU) and multiple I/O expanders.
elements.sent-2411-042: The whole system has been carefully optimized to reliably manage high-dynamic acoustic signals originating from various acoustic facilities and mobile platforms.
elements.sent-2411-043: We carried out comprehensive experiments in tank and lake environments to evaluate the performance of the proposed work across various configurations.
elements.sent-2411-044: The experimental results show that the UA-RIS can flexibly enhance or attenuate the strength of acoustic waves in specific directions.
elements.sent-2411-045: By deploying the UA-RIS with 24 reflection units at the sender side, the data rate of acoustic communication can be improved by an average of 63.8\% (up to 96\%), or the communication rage can be extended by 28\% and 46\% in deep water and shadow water environments, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-046: When positioned near the receiver, the UA-RIS extends the communication range in shallow and deep water environments by 40.6\% and 66\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-047: Additionally, situating the UA-RIS adjacent to the receiver boosts data rates by an average of 80.3\%, with increases up to 163\% under certain conditions.
elements.sent-2411-048: To summarize, our work makes three key contributions: \begin{adjustwidth {-0.71cm {0cm.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: We thoroughly investigate the implementation challenges of using RIS technology to achieve long-range, high-speed, and environmentally friendly underwater acoustic communications.
elements.sent-2411-050: An IQ modulation scheme is developed for UA-RIS that allows to generation of reflected signals with arbitrary amplitude and phase shifts.
elements.sent-2411-051: This technique ensures precise steering of the reflected waves, directing them effectively while minimizing interference in unintended directions.
elements.sent-2411-052: Both tank and lake experiments are conducted to validate the feasibility of the UA-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-053: The results confirm that our system can significantly enhance the quality of receiving signals in underwater environments for long-range and high-speed acoustic communications.
elements.sent-2411-054: Section~\!\ref{sec:App explores two applications to motivate our work.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Section~\!\ref{sec:IQmod examines the benefits of using IQ modulation in UA-RIS.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Section~\!\ref{sec:Hardware provides a detailed description of the UA-RIS hardware design.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Lastly, Section~\!\ref{sec:PerEva presents an experimental validation of the system performance.}
elements.sent-2411-058: The advent of RF-RIS technology lies in the study of metamaterials, which are composed of periodic structures with scales that are smaller than the wavelength of interacting electromagnetic (EM) waves.
elements.sent-2411-059: These metamaterials can resonate with incident EM fields, consequently producing a specific EM response on the surface with a precise design of the surface architecture~\cite{munk2005frequency, engheta2006metamaterials .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Metasurfaces have the potential to minimize radar cross-sections (RCSs), thereby improving the stealthiness of aircraft and target objects.
elements.sent-2411-061: As studied in \cite{cui2014coding , each unit on the metasurface can be configured to reflect incident EM waves in one of two states: in-phase or out-of-phase.}
elements.sent-2411-062: By combining these states across the surface with different patterns, it is possible to minimize the intensity of the backscattered signal.
elements.sent-2411-063: Over the past decade, metasurfaces have experienced significant evolution, especially in their enhanced ability to manipulate the phase and amplitude of incident waves.
elements.sent-2411-064: Such advancements have led to the development of RIS, capable of forming arbitrary reflected beams in space.
elements.sent-2411-065: This innovation plays a pivotal role in improving communication quality and is regarded as a promising technology for the next generation of wireless networks~\cite{hassouna2023survey, naeem2023security .}
elements.sent-2411-066: As introduced in \cite{boccia2002application , the phase response of a RIS can be electronically controlled by adding a varactor diode at the radiating edge of a patch antenna.}
elements.sent-2411-067: This technique enables each RIS unit to achieve a tunable phase range of up to 180\textdegree.
elements.sent-2411-068: Expanding upon this idea, \cite{hum2007modeling introduces a modification where microstrip patches are loaded with varactor diodes to enable broader beam forming.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Such a modification changes the resonant frequency of each reflector unit, thereby affecting the phase of the scattered signal.
elements.sent-2411-070: The RIS units developed in their work exhibited more than 320\textdegree\ of phase agility at 5.5\,GHz, while sustaining a reflection efficiency above 70\%.
elements.sent-2411-071: The architecture of the RIS presented in \cite{hum2007modeling has been applied in \cite{tan2016increasing to enhance spectrum sharing at the 2.4\,GHz frequency.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: This enhancement is realized by improving the link quality between communicating parties while canceling out interference to other network participants through the RF-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-073: The method involving the varactor to enable arbitrary phase shifts for extensive beamforming is further explored in \cite{pei2021ris .}
elements.sent-2411-074: Their study demonstrates that adjusting the varactor's bias voltage between 0\,V to 19\,V can produce a phase shift range from approximately $-$110\textdegree\ to 110\textdegree\ at 5.8\,GHz, while maintaining reflection efficiency above 70\%.
elements.sent-2411-075: In an indoor setting, the implemented RIS can overcome a 30\,cm thick concrete barrier and achieve a 26\,dB gain at the receiver.
elements.sent-2411-076: For outdoor applications, the system delivered a 14\,dB gain, facilitating high-speed communications across a distance of 500 meters.
elements.sent-2411-077: In \cite{abeywickrama2020intelligent , a practical phase shift model is proposed for RIS.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Diverging from traditional models that typically assume the RIS can alter the phase of reflected waves between $-\pi$ and $\pi$, while maintaining a constant amplitude of 1, the new model takes into account the coupling between the amplitude and the phase shift of reflected waves, aligning with the experimental results presented in \cite{zhu2013active .}
elements.sent-2411-079: This refined model is advantageous for designing optimal reflection coefficients for RIS, as it can balance between the amplitude and the phase of signals reflected by each unit.
elements.sent-2411-080: This ensures that the aggregate of the signals reflected towards the receiver has the maximum SNR.
elements.sent-2411-081: Inspired by achievements of metasurface in radio applications, the underwater acoustics sector is progressively embracing this new technology for its ability to manipulate the pattern of reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-082: These artificial structures demonstrate proficiency in minimizing the scattering cross-section (SCS) of sonar systems.
elements.sent-2411-083: Such an advancement is of strategic importance, especially for enhancing stealth properties in military applications.
elements.sent-2411-084: An example of the above application is the 2-bit coding metasurface developed in \cite{yu2021underwater .}
elements.sent-2411-085: This metasurface features four distinct types of elements.
elements.sent-2411-086: Each element is a steel plate punctured with four square holes at different depths, specifically engineered to shift the phase of reflected waves by 0, $\pi/2$, $\pi$, and $3\pi /2$.
elements.sent-2411-087: Through a meticulous arrangement of these elements, the design successfully attenuates the intensity of backscattered waves by at least 10\,dB, proving particularly effective within the 14.3\,kHz to 40.5\,kHz frequency range.
elements.sent-2411-088: Beyond metamaterials, conventional piezoelectric ceramics can also electronically manipulate the phase and amplitude of reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-089: For instance, the research conducted in \cite{eid2023enabling showcases the development of an underwater backscatter array designed for long-distance passive acoustic communication.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Utilizing the Van Atta array architecture, this system enables the reflected acoustic waves to automatically align with the direction of the incoming waves, eliminating the need for active components within the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-091: According to experimental data, the Van Atta backscatter array is capable of achieving a round-trip communication range exceeding 300 meters with a bit error rate (BER) of $10^{-3}$.
elements.sent-2411-092: The design of a UA-RIS for high-data-rate and long-range communication is explored in \cite{sun2022high .}
elements.sent-2411-093: Each unit of the proposed UA-RIS comprises a piezoelectric coil sandwiched between two metal plates to reflect acoustic waves.
elements.sent-2411-094: The resonant impedance of the piezoelectric reflector is adjusted using a varactor and an MCU, allowing for continuous tuning of the acoustic unit's elasticity.
elements.sent-2411-095: This adjustment introduces additional phase shifts in reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-096: The study in \cite{wang2023designing investigates wide-band beamforming using the UA-RIS architecture developed in \cite{sun2022high .}}
elements.sent-2411-097: This approach aims to neutralize two types of dispersion effects: element dispersion within each piezoelectric reflector and array dispersion across the acoustic RIS.
elements.sent-2411-098: By doing so, the directivity of the reflected waves becomes frequency-independent, allowing wideband signals to achieve consistent beamforming results across various frequency components.
elements.sent-2411-099: The effectiveness of the proposed architecture is assessed using COMSOL and the Bellhop simulator.
elements.sent-2411-100: In this section, we introduce two potential applications of using UA-RIS for achieving ultra-long-distance and environmentally friendly underwater acoustic communications.
elements.sent-2411-101: In oceans, underwater wildlife, such as dolphins and whales, depend critically on acoustic signals for communication, hunting, and navigation~\cite{luo2014challenges .}
elements.sent-2411-102: As shown in Fig.~\!\ref{fig:SpecShar , bottlenose dolphins use frequencies from 200\,Hz to 24\,kHz for whistles and 200\,Hz to 150\,kHz for echolocation clicks, while killer whales' echolocation typically occurs within the 12\,kHz to 25\,kHz range~\cite{richardson2013marine .}}
elements.sent-2411-103: These frequencies coincide with those used by human-made acoustic devices, potentially leading to disruptions or even damage to the auditory systems of marine life.
elements.sent-2411-104: Consequently, ensuring the coexistence of marine wildlife is an essential factor in designing next-generation underwater communication systems.
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=8.7cm]{figures/SpecShar}} \caption{Typical channel usage of marine life and human-made acoustic systems.}\label{fig:SpecShar}
elements.sent-2411-105: The UA-RIS technology offers a viable solution to achieve the aforementioned goal.
elements.sent-2411-106: With the assistance of RIS, acoustic modems can transmit data at a lower sound level, reducing their environmental footprint, and thereby minimizing the interference of sound devices with the acoustic channel.
elements.sent-2411-107: Furthermore, UA-RIS directs the reflected signal towards a specific direction rather than dispersing them throughout the space.
elements.sent-2411-108: This approach not only diminishes interference but also improves channel utilization in the spatial domain.
elements.sent-2411-109: For long-range communication, traditional underwater acoustic systems usually operate at low frequencies to mitigate propagation attenuation.
elements.sent-2411-110: This approach often requires either larger acoustic transducers or increased transmission power, which in turn shortens battery life.
elements.sent-2411-111: In contrast, UA-RIS provides an alternative by allowing the use of compact, low-power transducers for effective long-range communication effectively.
elements.sent-2411-112: To achieve the above objective, multiple UA-RIS units are strategically deployed at both the ocean's surface and its depths.
elements.sent-2411-113: As illustrated in Fig.~\!\ref{fig:Scenario , the surface UA-RIS unit is solar-powered and mounted on a buoy, while the deep-sea UA-RIS is connected to a wirewalker~\cite{del2021wirewalker , a fully passive device that harvests energy from the motion of ocean waves.}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=8.5cm]{figures/Scenario}} \caption{UA-RIS assisted long-range underwater acoustic communication network.}\label{fig:Scenario}
elements.sent-2411-114: By meticulously designing the system, UA-RIS units precisely direct the source signal toward the intended receiver during transmission.
elements.sent-2411-115: This approach coherently combines multiple acoustic waves reflected by the UA-RIS units at the receiver side, thereby substantially boosting the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).
elements.sent-2411-116: As a result, it facilitates extended communication ranges, even at higher frequencies, by effectively preserving signal strength over long distances.
elements.sent-2411-117: This section explores the IQ modulation technique utilized in our UA-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-118: We begin by examining the challenge of implementing arbitrary phase shifts within UA-RIS, followed by a description of how IQ modulation addresses this issue.
elements.sent-2411-119: Subsequently, we discuss the benefits of IQ modulation, highlighting its feasibility, flexibility, and interference reduction.
elements.subsection-2411-005: Challenges of Implementing Arbitrary Phase-Shift in UA-RIS
elements.sent-2411-120: In an ideal RIS, each reflecting unit should independently and passively control both the amplitude and phase of reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-121: However, implementing this functionality presents challenges in practical systems due to hardware constraints.
elements.sent-2411-122: As a result, most existing RF-RIS designs maintain a fixed amplitude and focus on modifying the phase of the reflected wave.
elements.sent-2411-123: This is typically achieved by adjusting the load impedance of each reflection unit using varactor diodes \cite{pei2021ris, abeywickrama2020intelligent .}
elements.sent-2411-124: Unfortunately, this approach is not viable for UA-RIS due to the low frequency of acoustic signal, as will be analyzed next.
elements.sent-2411-125: When a wave propagates through mediums with discontinuous impedances, reflection occurs.
elements.sent-2411-126: The reflection coefficient, denoted by $\Gamma$, is calculated using: \begin{equation}\label{eq:0aw2} \Gamma = \displaystyle\frac{Z_L-Z_0}{Z_L+Z_0},\end{equation} where $Z_L$ represents the load impedance, and $Z_0$ is the characteristic impedance of the transmission line.
elements.sent-2411-127: According to equation (\ref{eq:0aw2 ), an RIS with a characteristic impedance of 50\,$\Omega$, typical for RF transmission lines, requires load impedances of $-121j\,\Omega$ and $-50j\,\Omega$ to generate reflected waves with the same amplitude as the incident wave but with phase shifts of $-45^\circ$ and $-90^\circ$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Assuming a signal frequency of 30\,GHz is selected for mmWave communication, these load impedances correspond to capacitances of approximately 44\,pF and 106\,pF, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-129: Commercial varactor diodes, such as the ZC836A~\cite{830ser2007zetex and NTE618~\cite{nte2012nte , can continuously vary capacitance within this range by adjusting the reverse bias voltage applied to their p-n junctions, thereby enabling arbitrary phase shifts of the reflected waves.}}
elements.sent-2411-130: However, the frequencies used for middle-range or long-range acoustic communications are typically low, often below 50\,kHz.
elements.sent-2411-131: Under these conditions, the capacitance of the varactor diode needs to change significantly to shift the phase of reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-132: For example, maintaining the conditions from the previous analysis but reducing the signal frequency to 50\,kHz, the load network in the RIS would need to adjust the capacitance from 26 nF to 64 nF to shift the phase of the incident wave from $-45^\circ$ to $-90^\circ$.
elements.sent-2411-133: This range exceeds the capabilities of most commercial varactor diodes.
elements.sent-2411-134: Consequently, a new method is essential to implement arbitrary phase shifts for UA-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-135: In the proposed UA-RIS, we employ IQ modulation to generate reflected waves with arbitrary amplitude and phase shift.
elements.sent-2411-136: To implement this scheme, the MCU automatically pairs acoustic reflectors according to the angles of reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-137: Subsequently, one reflector in each pair produces an in-phase or antiphase component, shifting the phase of the reflected waves by $0$\textdegree\ or $180$\textdegree.
elements.sent-2411-138: The other reflector generates a quadrature component, shifting the phase of the reflected waves by $90$\textdegree\ relative to the incident signal.
elements.sent-2411-139: By precisely controlling the amplitude of the in-phase, antiphase, and quadrature components, the UA-RIS can synthesize reflected waves with any desired phase in space, as dictated by trigonometric identities: \begin{equation}\label{eq:js23} \begin{array}{lll} \sin(\omega t+\phi) \!\!\!=\!\!\! \sin\phi\cos\omega t + \cos\phi\sin\omega t \!\!\!\triangleq\!\!\! A_I \cos\omega t + A_Q\sin\omega t, \end{array}\end{equation} where $\omega$ is angular frequency; $t$ is time; $\phi$ is arbitrary phase shift between $-\frac{\pi {2}$ and $\frac{\pi {2}$; $A_I$ is the amplitude of in-phase ($A_I\!\geq\!0$) or antiphase component($A_I\!<\!0$); $A_Q$ is the amplitude of the quadrature component.}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq:js23} {lll} \sin(\omega t+\phi) \!\!\!=\!\!\! \sin\phi\cos\omega t + \cos\phi\sin\omega t \!\!\!\triangleq\!\!\! A_I \cos\omega t + A_Q\sin\omega t, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-140: Fig.~\!\ref{fig:IQmod illustrates how IQ modulation operates.}
elements.sent-2411-141: In the diagram, each circle represents an acoustic reflector, the size of which is one wavelength to efficiently reflect the incident waves.
elements.sent-2411-142: In the figure, the green plane indicates the direction of the incident wave, while the orange plane designates the target direction for the reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-143: To execute IQ modulation, only reflectors located on the wavefront of the reflected wave (as marked by the black dotted line in the figure) can be paired.
elements.sent-2411-144: For example, reflector 1 can be paired with reflector 2 or 2$^\prime$.
elements.sent-2411-145: With this configuration, the projection positions of the paired reflectors along the direction of the reflected waves align.
elements.sent-2411-146: Consequently, positional differences between paired reflectors do not result in additional phase shifts in the direction of the reflected wave.
elements.sent-2411-147: This setup enables the paired reflectors to function collectively as a single reflector, facilitating precise beam steering.
elements.sent-2411-148: It is important to recognize that the phase of the incident wave received by each reflector in a pair may differ.
elements.sent-2411-149: This discrepancy arises because the projection positions of the paired reflectors along the wavefront of the incident signals do not always align, as depicted in Fig.~\!\ref{fig:IQmod , where the green dotted line (representing the wavefront of the incident wave) is not parallel to the black dotted line.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Therefore, the UA-RIS must correct these phase differences to ensure that the reflected waves generated by the pair of reflectors remain orthogonal, which is crucial for effective IQ modulation.
elements.sent-2411-151: To address the aforementioned issue, the UA-RIS must tailor the reflection coefficients of each reflector based on the angle of the incident wave.
elements.sent-2411-152: Taking the pair of reflectors 1 and 2 in Fig.~\!\ref{fig:IQmod as an example, assume the incident waves received by reflector 1 and reflector 2 are $\cos(\omega t+\phi_1)$ and $\cos(\omega t+\phi_2)$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-153: The MCU then adjusts the impedance of the load network to set the reflection coefficient of reflectors 1 and 2 to $A_1$ and $jA_2$, respectively, where $j$ represents the imaginary component with $90^\circ$ phase shift.
elements.sent-2411-154: This configuration results in the sum of the acoustic waves reflected by reflectors 1 and 2 being: \begin{equation}\label{eq:8a2h} \begin{aligned} A_1\cos(\omega t+\phi_1) \!+\! A_2\cos(\omega t+\frac{\pi}{2}+\phi_2) = \;\;\;\left(A_1\cos\phi_1 + A_2\sin\phi_2\right)\cos\omega t +\!\left(A_1\sin\phi_1 + A_2\cos\phi_2\right)\sin\omega t. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Comparing (\ref{eq:8a2h ) with (\ref{eq:js23 ) reveals that $\left(A_1\cos\phi_1+A_2\sin\phi_2\right)$ is the amplitude of the in-phase or antiphase component, while $\left(A_1\sin\phi_1 + A_2\cos\phi_2\right)$ is the amplitude of the quadrature components for IQ modulation.}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:8a2h}  A_1\cos(\omega t+\phi_1) \!+\! A_2\cos(\omega t+\frac{\pi}{2}+\phi_2) = \;\;\;\left(A_1\cos\phi_1 + A_2\sin\phi_2\right)\cos\omega t +\!\left(A_1\sin\phi_1 + A_2\cos\phi_2\right)\sin\omega t. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-155: To direct the reflected signal towards the desired direction, assume that the waveform synthesized by reflectors 1 and 2 is represented as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:nc3t} \cos(\omega t+\phi_r) = \cos\phi_r\cos\omega t + \sin\phi_r\sin\omega t,\end{equation} where $\phi_r \!\in\! [-\frac{\pi {2 , \frac{\pi {2 ]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Then, by combining (\ref{eq:8a2h ) and (\ref{eq:nc3t ), the coefficients $A_1$ and $A_2$ of the reflectors 1 and 2 can be derived as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:kas4} A_1= \frac{ \begin{vmatrix} \cos\phi_r \sin\phi_2 \sin\phi_r \cos\phi_2 \end{vmatrix}} {\begin{vmatrix} \cos\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 \sin\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \end{vmatrix} }\, \quad \text{and} \quad A_2= \frac{ \begin{vmatrix} \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_r \sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_r \end{vmatrix}} {\begin{vmatrix} \cos\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 \sin\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \end{vmatrix} } ,\end{equation} where $|\,\mathbf{A}\,|$ indicates the determinant of a matrix $\mathbf{A}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Using (\ref{eq:kas4 ), the MCU can calculate the reflection coefficient for each reflector based on the angles of the incident and reflected waves.}
elements.sent-2411-158: This enables the synthesis of reflected waves with arbitrary phases, facilitating precise beam steering as required.
elements.sent-2411-159: This section outlines the hardware design of UA-RIS, beginning with an introduction to the structure of the acoustic reflector and the circuitry designed for efficient IQ modulation.
elements.sent-2411-160: Following this, we will discuss several critical considerations essential for real-world implementation.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Structure and Circuit of Acoustic Reflector
elements.sent-2411-161: Our work concentrates on acoustic signals within the mid-frequency range between 10\,kHz and 50\,kHz.
elements.sent-2411-162: As a result, the Tonpilz structure is selected for constructing the reflection unit, due to its superior electroacoustic conversion efficiency and effective impedance matching between the piezoelectric materials and water.
elements.sent-2411-163: This configuration allows for the flexible adjustment of the amplitude and phase of reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-164: Fig.~\!\ref{fig:Reflector depicts the structure of the reflector used in our UA-RIS, consisting of a head mass, two PZT disks equipped with electrodes, a prestressing rod, and a tail mass.}
elements.sent-2411-165: The head mass, a round cone cast from aluminum alloy, ensures effective oscillation along the central axis without placing undue stress on any component of the reflector.
elements.sent-2411-166: Situated behind the head mass are two PZT disks, strung together with a prestressing rod and separated by copper sheets that link to the subsequent circuit to regulate the reflector's load impedance.
elements.sent-2411-167: The tail mass at the rear reflects backward-traveling vibrations, ensuring that the acoustic wave is projected forward.
elements.sent-2411-168: In practical applications, achieving sufficient directional gain for a high-performance UA-RIS typically necessitates a substantial array of acoustic reflectors, impacting the size and cost of the system.
elements.sent-2411-169: Consequently, our UA-RIS is equipped with only two PZT plates per reflector, each with 38\,mm in diameter and 5\,mm in height.
elements.sent-2411-170: This build significantly reduces size, wight, and cost, facilitating widespread deployment of UA-RIS in marine environments.
elements.sent-2411-171: Fig.~\!\ref{fig:Circuit presents the circuit architecture of the proposed UA-RIS, consisting three primary components: a matching network, a load network, and a control \& processing unit.}
elements.sent-2411-172: Next, we will delve into a detailed explanation of each component.
elements.sent-2411-173: In the circuit, the matching network aligns the impedance of all reflectors to a real number, which is represented as $Z_0$ in (\ref{eq:0aw2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-174: Subsequently, the reflection coefficient for each reflector is modulated by linking different stages of the load network to the matching circuit.
elements.sent-2411-175: The connection of each stage is controlled through a pair of n-type metal-oxide-semiconductor (NMOS) transistors, whose ON/OFF states are regulated by TCA9555 I/O extenders and an ATmega256RFR2 MCU.
elements.sent-2411-176: The load network comprises three subnetworks: a resistor network, a capacitive network, and an inductive network.
elements.sent-2411-177: The resistor network has only one stage designed to generate the in-phase or antiphase component required for IQ modulation.
elements.sent-2411-178: Conversely, both the capacitive and inductive networks feature three stages each, dedicated to producing the quadrature component for IQ modulation.
elements.sent-2411-179: Resistor network contains an AD8403AR50 programmable potentiometer, whose resistance is managed by the MCU via the serial peripheral interface (SPI) protocol.

elements.sent-2411-183: According to (\ref{eq:0aw2 ), when $Z_0$ is a real number, the load impedance must be a complex number to generate the quadrature component necessary for IQ modulation.}
elements.sent-2411-184: In this scenario, it is impossible to continuously adjust the magnitude of the reflection coefficient by simply altering the resistance of the load impedance.
elements.sent-2411-185: To tackle this issue, in our design, the capacitive network includes three stages, designated as $Z_1$ to $Z_3$ in Fig.~\!\ref{fig:Circuit .}
elements.sent-2411-186: Connecting different stages of this network to the matching circuit yields a reflection coefficient with a consistent phase of $-$90\textdegree\ and three selectable amplitudes increasing stepwise from 0.3 to 0.9.
elements.sent-2411-187: Conversely, $Z_4$ to $Z_6$ form a three-stage inductive network that produces a reflection coefficient with a consistent phase of 90\textdegree, and three optional amplitudes ranging from 0.3 to 0.9.
elements.sent-2411-188: With the architecture depicted in Fig.~\!\ref{fig:Circuit , the MCU can effectively manipulate both the phase and magnitude of the reflection coefficient by adjusting the resistance of the potentiometer and the ON/OFF status of the NMOS transistors in each stage of the load network.}
elements.sent-2411-189: In the following section, we will introduce the selection of $Z_0$ and discuss the stratege for managing the high dynamic range of the acoustic signal in the circuit design.
elements.sent-2411-190: In the design of the load network for UA-RIS, we match the reflector's impedance to 1\,k$\Omega$ (i.e., $Z_0\!=\!1$\,k$\Omega$), a value substantially higher than the standard $50\,\Omega$ or $75\,\Omega$ commonly employed in radio communication systems.
elements.sent-2411-191: This significant divergence is attributed to the inherent resistance introduced by the wiper of the programmable potentiometer.
elements.sent-2411-192: In an ideal potentiometer, the resistance between a terminal and the wiper should vary continuously from 0 to $R_{max}$, where $R_{max}$ is the resistance between the two terminals.
elements.sent-2411-193: However, in a real programmable potentiometer, an array of MOSFETs is usually employed to emulate the function of a mechanical wiper for resistance adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-194: These MOSFETs inherently exhibit a certain level of resistance.
elements.sent-2411-195: In order to precisely control the phase and amplitude of the quadrature component for accurate IQ modulation, the wiper resistance of programmable potentiometers cannot be ignored.
elements.sent-2411-196: For instance, models such as the AD8403~\cite{analog2010channel and AD5245~\cite{analog2022position typically exhibit a wiper resistance of about 50\,$\Omega$, while the MAX5427 has a resistance of 100\,$\Omega$~\cite{maxim2005one .}}}

elements.sent-2411-199: As a result, the reflector will be incapable of generating an antiphase wave.
elements.sent-2411-200: To address the aforementioned problem, we match the impedance of the acoustic reflector to 1\,k$\Omega$, significantly higher than the inherent wiper resistance of the programmable potentiometer.
elements.sent-2411-201: This configuration allows the MCU to flexibly adjust the amplitude of the quadrature component between $-$1 and 1 by programming the resistance of the potentiometer.
elements.sent-2411-202: In practical scenarios, the distance between the UA-RIS and the acoustic transmitter may be unknown or can change over time in mobile applications.
elements.sent-2411-203: As a result, the intensity of the acoustic waves reaching the UA-RIS can vary significantly.
elements.sent-2411-204: To ensure the reliability of the UA-RIS, it is crucial that the hardware performs consistently across environments with both high and low energy densities.
elements.sent-2411-205: Acoustic signals naturally consist of periodic compressions and rarefactions within the medium.
elements.sent-2411-206: Consequently, the output from a reflector is always alternating current (AC).
elements.sent-2411-207: Therefore, the electronic switches in a UA-RIS must be capable of handling bidirectional current flow.
elements.sent-2411-208: Taking NMOS transistors as an example, when the incident power is weak, a single MOSFET can be utilized as an electronic switch to control the ON and OFF states of the load.
elements.sent-2411-209: However, as the intensity of the incident power increases, the intrinsic body diode of the NMOS transistor conducts during the negative half-cycle of the signal.
elements.sent-2411-210: Under these conditions, the load remains connected for half the signal cycle even if the gate-to-source voltage of the transistor falls below a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-211: To mitigate the aforementioned issue, the we pairs MOSFETs as an electronic switch.
elements.sent-2411-212: As depicted in Fig.~\!\ref{fig:Circuit , the source terminal of the upper NMOS transistor is connected to the drain terminal of the lower one.}
elements.sent-2411-213: This arrangement allows the body diode of the lower transistor to effectively prevent any reverse current in the load network.
elements.sent-2411-214: However, this setup necessitates at least an additional transistor and a pull-down resistor, denoted by $R_{pd}$ in the figure, for each stage of the load network.

elements.sent-2411-216: This section evaluates the performance of the proposed UA-RIS through experiments.
elements.sent-2411-217: Initially, we examine the power consumption of the system.
elements.sent-2411-218: Subsequently, the results collected from both tank and lake environments are presented to demonstrate the viability of the UA-RIS in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-219: Although the UA-RIS does not transmit acoustic signals, it still requires an energy supply to maintain the operation of active components for IQ modulation.
elements.sent-2411-220: In our implementation, the power consumption of the system primarily originates from four components: the MCU, twelve I/O extenders, and six programmable potentiometers.
elements.sent-2411-221: In this section, we thoroughly analyze the power consumption of UA-RIS operating in various states.
elements.sent-2411-222: In the absence of target waves, the UA-RIS remains in standby mode to save the energy.
elements.sent-2411-223: In this mode, the current consumption for the ATmega256RFR2 MCU, TCA9555 I/O extender with high inputs, and AD8403AR50 potentiometer at a 2\,V supply voltage are 650\,nA, 500\,nA, and 5\,$\mu$A, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-224: Consequently, the total power consumption of the system is only 73.3\,$\mu$W.
elements.sent-2411-225: However, upon detection of acoustic signals, UA-RIS transitions to an active mode.
elements.sent-2411-226: In this scenario, the power consumption of the system is divided into two stages: (a) the impedance adjustment phase, and (b) the impedance maintenance phase.
elements.sent-2411-227: In the impedance adjustment phase, the MCU controls the I/O extender and AD8403AR50 potentiometer to finely tune the load impedance in alignment with the desired direction of the reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-228: In the worst case, all load impedances shift from reactance to resistance, necessitating concurrent programming of all potentiometers.
elements.sent-2411-229: To achieve this, the MCU sends three bytes of data (chip address + register address + output value) via the $\text{I ^2\text{C}$ protocol to manage the I/O extender for each channel.}
elements.sent-2411-230: Additionally, it transmits two bytes (channel ID and value) through the SPI protocol to set the resistance for each potentiometer.
elements.sent-2411-231: As a result, the MCU issues a total number of 72 bytes and 48 bytes through the $\text{I ^2\text{C}$ and SPI protocols, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-232: After completing the load configuration, the circuit transitions into the impedance maintenance phase.
elements.sent-2411-233: In this phase, the MCU can enter a sleep mode, while the I/O extender and potentiometer continue to operate, maintaining the states of electronic switches and load impedance during a signal period for beam steering.
elements.sent-2411-234: At this time, the power consumption of the UA-RIS lies between that of the standby mode and the first phase of the active mode.

elements.sent-2411-358: In this paper, we explored the design of UA-RIS to enhance the range and speed of underwater acoustic communications.
elements.sent-2411-359: We addressed several challenging issues critical to system implementation, including wide dynamic range and low frequency of acoustic signals.
elements.sent-2411-360: To optimize performance, the proposed UA-RIS strategically aligns reflection units based on the angles of incident waves and their intended directions, utilizing IQ modulation to shape the reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-361: This approach can precise control over the phase and amplitude of the signals, enabling advanced beam-steering capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-362: Results from tank experiments demonstrated the capability of acoustic reflectors to finely tune the phase and amplitude of signals by adjusting the load impedance.
elements.sent-2411-363: Additionally, we evaluated a UA-RIS setup comprising 24 reflection units during lake trials, which proved effective in significantly enhancing or diminishing signal strength in designated directions.
elements.sent-2411-364: This adjustment improved receiving SNR and minimized interference at specific locations.
elements.sent-2411-365: Experimental results suggest that, by deploying the UA-RIS close to the sender, the communication range in deep and shallow water environments can be extended by 28\% and 46\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-366: When the distance between communicating parties remains constant, data transmission rates employing modulation schemes like FSK or PSK can be increased by an average of 63.8\%, and in some cases, up to 96\% with the support of UA-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-367: In addition, moiving the UA-RIS near the receiver extends the communication range in both deep and shallow water environments by 40.6\% and 66\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-368: Furthermore, with the distance fixed, situating the UA-RIS near the receiver not only improves data rates by an average of 80.3\% but can also achieve increases of up to 163\% under specific conditions.

elements.sent-2411-441: To enhance simulation accuracy, a five-step procedure is followed.
elements.sent-2411-442: First, the impedance of the PZT disk is determined by measuring the ratio of open-circuit voltage to short-circuit current.
elements.sent-2411-443: Based on this impedance value, appropriate matching circuit is constructed.
elements.sent-2411-444: A load is then introduced, and the resulting voltage response under the plane wave excitation is recorded.
elements.sent-2411-445: This measured voltage, together with the reflection coefficients of the load, is used to compute the reflected voltage.
elements.sent-2411-446: Finally, with the incident wave deactivated, the computed reflected voltage is applied to the matching circuits, and the resulting pressure waveform is recorded to represent the signal reflected by the structure under the specified load conditions.

elements.sent-2411-448: As shown in the figure, at 112\,$\mu$s, the stress in the reflector with an open-circuit load reaches its negative peak, whereas the stress in the reflector with a short-circuit load reaches its positive peak.
elements.sent-2411-449: This observation is consistent with the theoretical analysis presented in Section~\!\ref{sec:MotiIQ , which indicates that reflector coefficients generated by open and short circuits have identical amplitudes but opposite phases.}
elements.sent-2411-450: In contrast, the stress in reflectors loaded with L0.9 and C0.9 is nearly zero at this time instant.

elements.sent-2411-479: In the lake tests, using CW signals primarily validate steady-state characteristics such as beam steering and reflection capabilities of UA-RIS, which served as foundational assessments for this work.
elements.sent-2411-480: Practical modulation schemes that commonly used for underwater acoustic communications, such as frequency shift keying (FSK), phase shift keying, and orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) will introduce additional complexities and transient behaviors not fully captured by CW testing.
elements.sent-2411-481: However, preliminary analytical and experimental results collected in the field test provide strong evidence that performance improvements are expected when transitioning to practical modulation schemes.
elements.sent-2411-482: To ensure optimal performance, real-time attitude estimation can be achieved by incorporating data from an inertial measurement unit (IMU) and a pressure sensor.
elements.sent-2411-483: For instance, the gyroscope within the IMU provides angular velocity measurements that enable tracking of three-dimensional orientation changes over time, while the pressure sensor supplies depth information essential for vertical positioning.
elements.sent-2411-484: Accurate estimation of the angle of incident waves for steering the reflected signal in the desired direction is essential yet challenging tasks.
elements.sent-2411-485: To solve this problem, UA-RIS can leverage the spatial characteristics of the received signal across the reflection elements to estimate the angle of arrival (AoA) of an incident signal.
elements.sent-2411-486: In scenarios where the RIS is equipped with sensing capabilities either through embedded low-power sensors or via hybrid active elements, it becomes possible to treat the RIS as an antenna array that samples the incoming wavefront.
elements.sent-2411-487: By modeling the received signal as a superposition of array response vectors parameterized by the incident angle, traditional array signal processing techniques such as MUSIC (multiple signal classification) or ESPRIT (estimation of signal parameters via rotational invariance techniques) can be applied to estimate the AoA with high resolution.
elements.sent-2411-488: This approach allows precise directional information to be extracted, which is critical for dynamically configuring the RIS reflection pattern to optimize signal propagation and enhance communication performance.
elements.sent-2411-489: To optimize performance, the proposed UA-RIS strategically aligns reflection units based on the angles of incident waves and their intended directions, utilizing \textcolor{black {synthetic reflection method to shape the reflected waves.}}
elements.sent-2411-490: Results from \textcolor{black {COMSOL Multiphysics simulation and tank experiments demonstrated the capability of acoustic reflectors to finely tune the phase and amplitude of signals by adjusting the load impedance.}}
elements.sent-2411-491: Experimental results \textcolor{black {using a CW signal as the source indicate that deploying the UA-RIS near the transmitter can extend the communication range by up to 28\% and 46\% in deep water and shallow water environments, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-492: In addition, moving the UA-RIS near the receiver extends the communication range in both deep and shallow water environments by 40.6\% and 66\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-493: Adapting these protocols \textcolor{black {with different modulation schemes (e.g., FSK, PSK, and OFDM) will enable the seamless integration of UA-RIS into established underwater network infrastructures, a topic we intend to investigate in our future research.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}}}
